prompt: Which choice best states the main idea of the passage?

choices.text: Writing an unsent letter helps Tomas privately acknowledge his homesickness.
choices.rationale: Correct. The closing lines show the unsent writing let him admit his homesickness.

choices.text: Tomas writes the letters mainly to impress the friends in his new city.
choices.rationale: The letters are addressed home, not aimed at impressing new friends.

choices.text: Tomas mails the most honest of his three letters to his family at last.
choices.rationale: He keeps the letter folded in a drawer rather than mailing it.

choices.text: Tomas decides he no longer misses his hometown now that he has moved.
choices.rationale: The passage shows he does miss home, not that he stopped.

explanation: The narrative resolves on writing as a way to admit feelings to himself.

stimulus.title: The Unsent Letter
stimulus.text: Tomas wrote the letter three times before he stopped trying to send it. In the first version he was cheerful, listing the small triumphs of his new city as if homesickness were a thing he had outgrown. In the second he was honest, and the page filled with everything he missed until it embarrassed him. The third version said almost nothing at all. He kept that one folded in a drawer. Months later he found it again and realized the words he could not send had still done their work: writing them had let him admit, if only to himself, how far from home he truly felt.

prompt: Which choice best states the central idea of the passage?

choices.text: Salt marshes release large amounts of stored carbon into the warming air.
choices.rationale: The roots store carbon rather than releasing it.

choices.text: Salt marshes are beautiful landscapes that people have always admired greatly.
choices.rationale: The passage calls the marsh drab and once-overlooked, not always admired.

choices.text: Draining marshes is the most effective way to defend homes from storms.
choices.rationale: Draining marshes removed protection rather than providing it.

choices.text: Plain-looking salt marshes provide valuable protection that was long overlooked.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage shows the drab marsh doing protective work long unappreciated.

explanation: The passage reveals the overlooked marsh as a valuable natural protector.

stimulus.title: The Quiet Buffer
stimulus.text: Where the land meets the sea, a salt marsh can look like wasteland: flat, muddy, and crossed by winding channels. Yet this drab landscape does remarkable work. Its dense grasses slow incoming waves, blunting the force of storms before they reach homes inland. The same grasses trap sediment, letting the marsh build itself upward as the sea slowly rises. Beneath the mud, roots store carbon that might otherwise warm the air. For years, marshes were drained and filled without much thought. Only later did people recognize that the unglamorous marsh had been protecting them all along, asking nothing in return.

prompt: Which choice best states the central idea of the passage?

choices.text: Mathematicians decided that zero should be removed from written calculations.
choices.rationale: Mathematicians embraced zero, rather than removing it.

choices.text: Zero was always understood as an ordinary number throughout all of history.
choices.rationale: The passage says zero was long absent and unsettling, not always understood.

choices.text: Treating zero as a true number transformed how people could write and calculate.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage shows how recognizing zero as a number reshaped writing and calculation.

choices.text: Ancient people were unable to count sheep or coins without using zero.
choices.rationale: The passage says people counted sheep and coins easily without zero.

explanation: The passage's point is that accepting zero as a number transformed mathematics.

stimulus.title: The Number That Was Nothing
stimulus.text: For much of history, there was no symbol for nothing. People counted sheep and coins easily enough, but the idea of writing down an empty amount seemed pointless, even unsettling. The breakthrough came when mathematicians treated zero not as the absence of a number but as a number in its own right, one that could be added, subtracted, and used to hold a place in larger figures. With zero, the difference between 5 and 50 could be written clearly, and long calculations grew far simpler. A concept that once felt like a riddle became the quiet hinge on which much of modern mathematics turns.

prompt: Which choice best states the central idea of the passage?

choices.text: Bats rely chiefly on their sharp eyesight to hunt insects after dark.
choices.rationale: The passage describes hunting by sound, not eyesight.

choices.text: Bats can detect only objects that are completely still, never moving ones.
choices.rationale: Bats detect motion through shifts in the returning sound.

choices.text: Bats produce sounds that humans can easily hear during the night.
choices.rationale: The calls are often too high for humans to hear.

choices.text: Bats perceive their surroundings in the dark by interpreting echoes of their own calls.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage explains how bats build a sound-based picture from echoes.

explanation: The passage centers on echolocation as a sound-built perception of the world.

stimulus.title: Seeing With Sound
stimulus.text: A bat hunting in pitch darkness does not bump into things. It calls out, often at pitches too high for humans to hear, and listens for the echoes that bounce back. From the delay between call and echo, the bat judges how far away an object is; from subtle shifts in the returning sound, it can tell whether the object is moving toward it or away. A moth's fluttering wing returns a different echo than a still leaf. So precise is this sense that some bats can snatch an insect from the air in total darkness. The world the bat perceives is built entirely of sound, a picture painted with echoes.

prompt: Which choice best states the central idea of the passage?

choices.text: European merchants invented spices and were the first ever to harvest them.
choices.rationale: Merchants traded spices; they did not invent or first harvest them.

choices.text: Sailors avoided long voyages because the spice trade was already too crowded.
choices.rationale: The high prices drove sailors to seek new routes, not avoid voyages.

choices.text: Black pepper has always been a cheap and easily available kitchen ingredient.
choices.rationale: The passage says spices were once nearly as valuable as silver.

choices.text: The high cost of distant spices spurred voyages that eventually made spices common.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage links costly, rare spices to voyages that made them ordinary.

explanation: The passage traces spices from costly rarity to abundance through new sea routes.

stimulus.title: The Price of Pepper
stimulus.text: There was a time when a handful of black pepper could rival a handful of silver. The spice grew only in distant lands, and by the time it reached a European market it had passed through many hands, each adding a markup. Merchants guarded their routes jealously and spread tall tales about how spices were harvested, partly to discourage rivals. The high prices drove sailors to seek their own sea paths to the source, voyages that redrew the map of the world. In the end, the very abundance those voyages produced made spices ordinary, and the fortunes built on rarity quietly dissolved.

prompt: Which choice best states the central idea of the passage?

choices.text: Lichens are the only living things able to survive on exposed rock surfaces.
choices.rationale: The passage does not claim lichens are the only rock survivors.

choices.text: Lichens compete with one another for the limited sunlight on a rock.
choices.rationale: Competition among lichens is not the focus.

choices.text: Lichens prepare bare rock for later plants by helping form the first soil.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage shows lichens building the soil that lets later plants grow.

choices.text: Mosses and small plants arrive on bare rock before any lichen does.
choices.rationale: The passage says lichens come first and mosses follow.

explanation: The passage frames the lichen as a pioneer that makes soil for what comes next.

stimulus.title: First on the Rock
stimulus.text: When a bare rock is left exposed, the first life to arrive is often a lichen, a strange partnership of fungus and alga living as one. The fungus grips the stone and gathers moisture; the alga, sheltered inside, makes food from sunlight. Together they need no soil at all. As a lichen grows, it slowly releases acids that crack the rock surface, and its dead tissue mixes with grit to form the first thin crumbs of soil. Years later, mosses and small plants can take root in that beginning. The lichen will likely be crowded out by the very community it made possible, an unsung founder of the green world that follows.

prompt: Which choice best states the central idea of the passage?

choices.text: Canal locks let boats overcome changes in elevation, opening inland trade.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage explains how locks conquer elevation and thereby expand trade.

choices.text: Rivers were once the fastest possible way to move goods over long distances.
choices.rationale: The passage does not rank rivers as the fastest route.

choices.text: Boats could always travel freely inland long before the lock was invented.
choices.rationale: The passage says elevation once blocked inland travel.

choices.text: Locks required rare and costly materials that few towns could ever afford.
choices.rationale: The passage stresses humble means, not rare materials.

explanation: The lock's purpose and its effect on inland commerce are the passage's focus.

stimulus.title: Stairs for Boats
stimulus.text: Rivers do not run level, and for centuries that simple fact blocked boats from traveling far inland. The lock changed things. A lock is a chamber with gates at each end; a boat enters, the gates close, and water is let in or drained out until the boat rises or falls to the next stretch of river. String several locks together, and a vessel can climb a hillside the way a person climbs a staircase. The engineering is humble, relying on nothing more exotic than gravity and patience, yet it knit distant towns into trading partners and turned shallow rivers into highways of commerce.

prompt: Which choice best states the main idea of the passage?

choices.text: Researchers prefer studying coral because it is easier than studying trees.
choices.rationale: No claim of relative ease over tree study is made.

choices.text: Warm water is the only factor that determines how thick a coral band grows.
choices.rationale: Chemistry and season both matter; warmth is not called the only factor.

choices.text: Coral grows faster than trees and so makes a more accurate calendar.
choices.rationale: The passage compares coral to trees but never claims it grows faster.

choices.text: Coral bands preserve a long, readable record of past ocean conditions.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage centers on how coral bands record and reveal past conditions.

explanation: The passage frames the coral as a centuries-long record that researchers learn to read.

stimulus.title: The Coral That Keeps Time
stimulus.text: A coral skeleton grows in fine bands, much like the rings of a tree. In bright, warm months the coral lays down thick layers; in cooler, dimmer months the layers thin. By slicing a coral and counting these bands, researchers can read a record of past seasons stretching back centuries. Even better, the chemistry trapped in each band hints at the water's temperature when it formed. So a single old coral can serve as a kind of diary, written not in words but in stone, recording conditions long before anyone thought to measure them. The challenge is learning to read a language no one designed to be read.
